data.title: Hierarchy-Aware Dependency Enforcement for the Ticket System
data.description:
## Context
Agents and humans run `ticket link` during sprint and fix-bug workflows to express that one piece of work blocks another. Today the system allows linking any ticket to any other regardless of ancestry — agents create cross-epic and cross-story sub-ticket dependency chains that are semantically meaningless (the actual constraint lives at the parent story or epic level). Additionally, tickets can be linked to their direct parent or child in the hierarchy, duplicating the parent-child relationship as a redundant dependency. Both patterns produce dep-graph noise that obscures real blocking relationships and causes agents to make incorrect scheduling decisions.

## Success Criteria
- SC1: ticket link task-A task-B blocks|depends_on where task-A and task-B share the same parent story → link written as-is (intra-story dependency, allowed).
- SC2: ticket link story-X story-Y blocks|depends_on where story-X and story-Y share the same parent epic → link written as-is (intra-epic story dependency, allowed).
- SC3: ticket link task-A task-B blocks|depends_on where task-A is under story-X (epic-P) and task-B is under story-Y (epic-P, same epic) → promoted to story-X → story-Y; redirect notice printed to stderr.
- SC4: ticket link story-X story-Y blocks|depends_on where story-X and story-Y are under different epics → promoted to epic-P → epic-Q; redirect notice to stderr.
- SC5: ticket link task-A task-B blocks|depends_on where the tasks are under different epics → promoted to epic-P → epic-Q; redirect notice to stderr.
- SC6: ticket link <any-ticket> <epic-B> blocks|depends_on where the ticket's root epic differs from epic-B → promoted to source-root-epic → epic-B; redirect notice to stderr.
- SC7: ticket link story-A epic-P blocks|depends_on (or the reverse) where epic-P is story-A's direct parent → exits non-zero with a redundant with hierarchy error message; no LINK event written.
- SC8: ticket link task-A story-X blocks|depends_on (or the reverse) where story-X is task-A's direct parent → exits non-zero with a redundant with hierarchy error message; no LINK event written.
- SC9: Attempting to create a circular or self-referential dependency at the redirected level (epic-to-epic or story-to-story, including self-loops where source and target resolve to the same ticket) → exits non-zero with a clear error; no LINK event written.
- SC10: Tickets with no parent_id (orphan tickets) → link written as-is; no redirect or error.
- SC11: If .cache.json is missing for any ticket encountered during hierarchy resolution, the operation fails with an explicit error rather than silently treating the ticket as an orphan.
- SC12: ticket_type values in .cache.json are compared case-insensitively during cycle detection scoping (handles "epic" and "Epic" variants).
- SC13: Hierarchy resolution reads at most 4 .cache.json files per ticket link call (at most 2 per ticket end, at most 2 hops up). Performance validated by a benchmark test running ticket link against a synthetic ticket tree of 1,000 tickets completing in under 5 seconds on a developer workstation.
- SC14: End-to-end dep-graph validation — given a synthetic ticket tree (1 epic, 2 stories, 2 tasks per story), running ticket link task-A task-B depends_on where task-A and task-B are under different stories of the same epic must produce: (a) ticket show story-X lists story-X to story-Y as a dependency in its deps output, and (b) ticket show task-A does NOT list task-A to task-B in its deps output. Both assertions verified by a sprint acceptance test run within the session.

## Scenario Analysis
- Parent-chain walk truncates on missing cache (high, runtime): If .cache.json is absent for an intermediate ticket, the walk stops early and silently misclassifies the link scope. Mitigated by SC11.
- BFS may load many epics (medium, runtime): With 200+ epics, loading all epic caches for BFS could approach limits on slow hardware. Mitigated by SC13 benchmark.
- Redirect notice lost in sub-agent context (medium, runtime): stderr is not surfaced to orchestrator context. Implementation should also emit machine-readable JSON to stdout when a redirect occurs.
- Existing non-compliant LINK events remain (high, deployment): Previously written cross-hierarchy links are not retroactively corrected. A migration/fsck utility is out of scope; tracked as a follow-on.
- ticket_type case mismatch skips cycle detection (high, configuration): Mistyped ticket_type in .cache.json silently skips BFS nodes. Mitigated by SC12.
- Grandparent self-loop not blocked by SC7/SC8 (medium, configuration): Task linking to its grandparent epic resolves to a self-loop. Mitigated by SC9 extended to cover self-loops.

## Dependencies
None

## Approach
Extend ticket-graph.py with two new functions: resolve_hierarchy_link(source_id, target_id, tracker_dir) walks each ticket's parent_id chain (at most 2 hops, at most 4 .cache.json reads total) to find the divergence point and returns the effective (source_id, target_id) pair plus was_redirected and is_redundant flags, failing explicitly on missing cache; check_cycle_at_level(source_id, target_id, level, tracker_dir) runs BFS scoped to tickets with ticket_type matching the target level (case-insensitive), detecting cycles and self-loops, bounded by level-ticket count not total ticket count. Modify ticket-link.sh to call these before writing LINK events: reject redundant links, redirect cross-boundary links, check cycles, write LINK event for resolved pair. IPC between shell and Python uses JSON stdout.
